pattern: A synchronized two-pointer walk over both sorted arrays only needs to reconcile running sums where the arrays touch the same value, since everywhere else the two paths cannot influence each other.
hints: Because both arrays are sorted and strictly increasing, a merge-style two-pointer walk lets you accumulate a running sum along each array independently between shared values.
When the pointers are looking at different values, advance whichever one is smaller and add that value to its own running sum.

When both pointers land on the same value, that is a mandatory rendezvous: keep only the larger of the two running sums, add the shared value once, and reset both sums to that combined total before continuing; the final answer is the larger total once both arrays are exhausted, modulo 1e9 + 7.

edge_inputs.description: one shared value splits both paths
no shared values, pick the larger sum outright
shared value at the very start forces an immediate merge
shared value at the very end

stress.description: large arrays sharing a random fraction of values

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def maxSum(self, nums1, nums2):
        import bisect
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        common = sorted(set(nums1) & set(nums2))
        def segment_sums(nums, points):
            prefix = [0]
            for v in nums:
                prefix.append(prefix[-1] + v)
            sums = []
            prev_idx = 0
            for p in points:
                pos = bisect.bisect_left(nums, p)
                sums.append(prefix[pos] - prefix[prev_idx])
                prev_idx = pos + 1
            sums.append(prefix[len(nums)] - prefix[prev_idx])
            return sums
        s1 = segment_sums(nums1, common)
        s2 = segment_sums(nums2, common)
        total = sum(max(a, b) for a, b in zip(s1, s2))
        total += sum(common)
        return total % MOD
